title: 第 43 節
en: If thou art delivered by the Spirit from manhood, Thou hast found eternal rest in the sanctuary of God;
He who has directed himself so that all passions are silent, Will surely, like Jesus, ascend to heaven.’
Abu Said Abul Cheir, Founder of Sufiism.
Rabia may be called a Sufi before even the rise of Sufiism. Her Sufiism seems quite her own, without any traces of foreion influence. The real founder, 944. LECTURE XI.
however, of the Sufis as a religious sect was Abu Said Abul Cheir, about 820 a. Ὁ.
Abu Yasid and Junaid.

Towards the end of the same century a schism took place, one party following Abu Yasid al-Bushani, whose pantheistic views were in open conflict with the Koran, the other following Junaid, who tried to reconcile Sufiism with orthodoxy. There were then, as at present, Sufis and Sufis. Some wrote in Persian, such as Sendai, Ferid eddin Attar, JellAl eddin Rimi (d. 1162), Jimi (d. 1172); others in Arabie, such as Omar ibn el Faridh, and Izz eddin Mutaddesi, others even in Turkish.

Some of their poetry is magnificent in imagery, and highly valued even by those who are afraid of the consequences of their doctrines. Sufiism was said to breed an alarming familiarity with the deity, and a disregard of human and divine ordinances, at least among those who have not reached the highest spiritual purity, and might be tempted to use their outward sanctity as a cloak for human frailty.
Sufi, Fakir, Darwish.

The etymology of Sufi, as derived from δ ἢ wool, because they walked about dressed in white woollen garments is now generally accepted!. Formerly it was supposed that Sufi came from the Greek σοφύς, which is impossible. At present the Sufis are generally known as Fakirs, in Persian as Darwish, i.e. poor. Formerly they were also called Arif, theosophist, and Ahl alyakyn, the veople of surety. Thus one of them, Abd al Razzak, 1 Sprenger, i. p. 262.

SUFILISM. 345 says: ‘All praise to Allah, who by His grace and favour has saved us from the researches of conventional sciences, who by the spirit of immediate intuition has lifted us above the tediousness of tradition and demonstration, who has removed us from the hollow threshing of straw, and kept us pure from disputation, opposition and contradiction ; for all this is the arena of uncertainty and the field of doubt, of error, and heresy; glory to Him who has taken away from our eyes the veil of externals, of form, and confusion.’
Asceticism.

The Sufis trust to the inward eye that is opened in raptures; and which, if it is weak or blind, can be helped on by ascetic discipline. This ascetic discipline was originally no more than abstaining from food and drink, and other pleasures of life. But it soon degenerated into wild fanaticism. Some of the Fakirs indulged in violent exercises intended to produce convulsions, cataleptic fits, and all the rest. The Darwishes, who may be seen now turning round and round till they break out in delirious shouts, are the degraded descendants of the Sufis. Attar and Jellal eddin Rtmi, like true lovers of God, required no stimulants for their enthusiasm, and their poetical genius found utterance, not in inarticulate ravings, but in enraptured hymns of praise. The true Sufis were always honoured, not only for their genius, but for their saint-like lives, and they could well bear comparison with their contemporaries in the West, even such as St. Bernard.
When speaking of the true and saint-like Sufis, Jellal eddin says :—

‘Faithful they are, but not for Paradise, God’s Will the only crowning of their faith: And not for seething Hell flee they from sin, But that their will must serve the Will divine. It is no struggle, ’tis not discipline Wins them a will so restful and so blest ;
It is that God from His heart-fountain core Fills up their jubilant soul.’

It is true there is little of what we call theosophic philosophy in their utterances. That belongs almost exclusively to the Vedantist, and to a certain extent to the Yogins also of India. The Sufi trusts to his feelings, nay, almost to his senses, not, as the VedAntist, to his philosophical insight. He has intuitions or beatifie visions of God, or he claims at least to have them. He feels the presence of God, and his highest blessedness on earth is the mystic union with God, of which he speaks under ever-varying, and sometimes, to us at least, startling imagery. Yet for his highest raptures he too confesses that human language has no adequate expression. As Sady says, the flowers which a lover of God had gathered in his rose-garden, and which he wished to give to his friends, so overpowered his mind by their fragrance, that they fell out of his lap and withered; that is to say, the glory of ecstatic visions pales and fades away when it has to be put into human language.
The Mesnevi.

6118] eddin in the Preface to his Mesnevi, says: ‘This book contains strange and rare narratives, beautiful sayings, and recondite indications, a path SUFIISM. O47 for the devout, and a garden for the pious, short in expressions, numerous in its applications. It contains the roots of the roots of the roots of the Faith, and treats of the mysteries of union and sure knowledge. This book is looked upon by Mohammedans as second only to the Koran, and yet it would be difficult to imagine two books more different one from the other.
Mohammed’s Opinion.

Mohammed’s idea of God is after all the same as that of the Old Testament. Allah is chiefly the God of Power; a transcendent, but a strongly personal God. He is to be feared rather than to be approached, and true religion is submission to His will (Islam). Even some of the Sufis seem to shrink from asserting the perfect oneness of the human and the divine natures. They call the soul divine, God-like, but not yet God; as if in this case the adjective could really be distinguished from the substantive, as if anything could be divine but God alone, and as if there could be even a likeness of God, or anything God-like, that was not in its essence God. Philosophical speculations on God were distasteful to Mohammed. ‘Think on the mercies of God, he says in one place, ‘not on the essence of God.’ He knew that theological speculation would inevitably lead to schism. ‘My people shall be divided,’ he says, ‘into three and seventy sects, of which all save one shall have their portion in the fire.’ That one with Mohammed would certainly not have been that of the Sufis.
There is an interesting poem in which Said, the servant, first recounts one morning an ecstasy he had 348 LECTURE XI.
enjoyed, and is then warned by Mohammed against excessive fervour:
Said speaks :

‘My tongue clave fever-dry, my blood ran fire, My nights were sleepless with consuming love, Till night and day sped past, as flies a lance, Grazing a buckler’s rim; a hundred thousand years No longer than a moment. In that hour All past eternity and all to come Was gathered up in one stupendous Now,—
Let understanding marvel as it may, Where men see clouds, on the ninth heaven I gaze, And see the throne of God. All heaven and hell Are bare to me and all men’s destinies.
The heavens and earth, they vanish at my glance, The dead rise at my look. I tear the veil From all the worlds, and in the hall of heaven I set me central, radiant as the sun.
Then spake the Prophet (Mohammed), Friend, thy steed is warm ;
Spur him no more. The mirror in thy heart Did slip its fleshly case, now put it up—
Hide it once more, or thou wilt come to harm.’
zh: 若汝為精神所拯救，脫離人之本性，汝已在上帝的聖殿中尋得永恆的安息；
他若自我引導使得一切熱情俱寂， 必定如耶穌一般升入天堂。
阿布‧賽義德‧阿布爾‧凱爾，蘇菲主義的創立者。
拉比亞可謂於蘇菲主義興起之前已是蘇菲派人士。她的蘇菲主義似乎完全源於自身，不帶任何外來影響的痕跡。真正的創始人，944。第十一講。
不過，蘇菲派作為一個宗教教派的創始人是阿布‧賽義德‧阿布爾‧凱爾（Abu Said Abul Cheir），大約在西元八二〇年。
阿布‧亞西德與朱奈德。

同一世紀末期，蘇非主義發生了分裂，一派追隨阿布‧亞西德‧布什蘭尼（Abu Yasid al-Bushani），其泛神論的觀點與《古蘭經》公開相悖；另一派追隨朱奈德，他試圖將蘇非主義與正統教義相調和。當時如同現在一樣，蘇非派中有各種不同的立場。有些人用波斯語著述，如森代、費里丁‧阿塔爾、傑拉勒丁‧魯米（卒於1162年）、吉米（卒於1172年）；有些人用阿拉伯語著述，如歐瑪爾‧伊本‧法里德和伊茲‧埃丁‧穆塔德西；還有人甚至用土耳其語著述。
他們有些詩歌在意象上宏偉壯麗，即使是那些害怕其教義後果的人，也對其推崇備至。蘇菲主義據說會導致與神明過於熟悉親暱的情況，以及對人事和神聖誡律的漠視，至少在那些尚未達到最高精神純淨境界的人當中是如此，他們可能會被誘惑將其外在的聖潔用作掩蓋人性脆弱的幌子。
蘇非派信徒、苦行僧、苦修者。

蘇菲（Sufi）的語源係衍生自δ（羊毛），因為他們身著白色羊毛衣袍而四處遊行，此說現已普遍被接受¹。從前曾有人認為蘇菲來自希臘文σοφύς，但此說乃不可能。當今蘇菲人通常被稱為苦行者（Fakirs），在波斯語中稱為達爾維什（Darwish），即「貧者」之義。從前他們也被稱為阿瑞夫（Arif），即神祕主義者，以及「確定之人」（Ahl alyakyn）。因此其中一位人物，阿卜杜·拉扎克（Abd al Razzak）¹ ¹ Sprenger, i. p. 262.
頁 345 云：「讚美真主，祂以恩典與眷顧拯救我們脫離常規科學的研究；祂以直接直覺之靈提升我們超越傳統與論證的乏味；祂將我們移離虛空的稻草脫粒，保守我們遠離爭論、反對與矛盾；蓋凡此皆乃不確定的競技場與懷疑之域、謬誤與異端之場所；榮耀歸於祂，祂自我們眼前揭去了外在之幕、形式與混淆之障蔽。」
禁欲主義

蘇菲派信徒依賴內在之眼，此眼在狂喜中開啟；若其衰弱或失明，可藉禁慾修行得以輔助。此禁慾修行原本不過是戒絕飲食及生命中的其他樂趣。然而它很快便墮落為狂野的狂熱主義。某些苦行僧縱情於暴烈的修行，意圖引發痙攣、僵直昏迷及其他種種現象。目下可見旋轉不止直至陷入譫妄呼喊的德爾維什（Darwishes），乃蘇菲派衰退的後裔。阿塔爾（Attar）與傑拉爾丁‧魯米（Jellal eddin Rumi）如同真摯的神之愛好者，其熱忱無需刺激，其詩歌天才得以表現，不在於無意義的狂亂呼號，而在於狂喜的讚美詩歌。真正的蘇菲派向來備受尊敬，不僅因其天才，亦因其聖潔的生活，彼等足可與西方同時代人相提並論，甚至如聖伯爾納德（St. Bernard）之輩亦然。
在談論真正且聖潔的蘇菲派信徒時，傑拉爾‧丁（Jellal eddin）說：——
'他們忠誠，但不是為了天堂，唯有上帝的旨意才是他們信仰的冠冕：他們逃避罪惡，也不是為了沸騰的地獄，而是因為他們的意志必須服侍神聖的旨意。這不是掙扎，也不是紀律為他們贏得了如此寧靜與蒙福的意志；
祂以心泉之源滿足他們歡悅的靈魂。

誠然，他們的言論中幾乎沒有我們所謂的神智哲學。那種哲學幾乎完全屬於吠檀多主義者，以及印度的瑜伽修行者在某種程度上。蘇非派信徒依賴的是他的感受，甚至幾乎是他的感官，而非如吠檀多主義者那樣依賴哲學洞察。他擁有關於上帝的直覺或極樂幻象，或至少聲稱擁有這些經驗。他感受到上帝的臨在，他在塵世間的最高福樂是與上帝的神祕合一，他用不斷變化的、有時對我們而言令人驚異的意象來談論這種合一。然而，即便在他最高的狂喜中，他也承認人類語言無法充分表達。如薩迪所言，一位上帝的愛慕者從他的玫瑰園裡采集的花朵，本欲贈予友人，卻因花香過於沁人心脾而令他的心神為之奪去，以至於花朵從他的膝上滑落，凋謝了；也就是說，當狂喜幻象必須轉化為人類語言時，它的光輝就褪去並消散了。
《梅斯內維》
傑拉魯丁在其《精神頌》的序言中說：「本書包含奇異而罕見的敍事、優美的言辭和隱微的指示，為虔誠者開闢一條道路，為敬虔者闢出一座花園，用語簡潔，應用繁多。它包含了信仰之根的根的根，並論述了合一與確知的奧祕。本書在穆罕默德信徒中的地位僅次於《古蘭經》，然而卻難以想像有兩部書會如此迥然不同。」

> When we read in the Koran that "there is no god but God," and that "Mohammed is the apostle of God," we recognise at once the watchword of a religion which, whatever its faults, was certainly meant to be, and has actually proved itself to be, a religion of the highest monotheism (一神教). It matters little whether we call such a religion pure monotheism (一神教) or strict henotheism (單一神教), provided we understand what we mean by these terms. The great point is that all the infinite varieties of divine powers, all the countless gods and goddesses of the ancient world, are here reduced to one. Whatever name we give to this supreme power—whether Alláh or God—it is recognised as one, eternal, unchangeable, and all-powerful. That a religion, starting with such a conception, should in after times have produced a system of saint-worship, approaching in many respects to the old polytheism (多神教), is no more singular than that Christianity itself should have developed in a similar direction. It would be difficult to say whether the growth of such aberrations is to be attributed more to the weakness of human nature or to the pride of the priesthood. But the original teaching of Mohammed on the subject of the divine nature admits of no compromise. It is strict, uncompromising, and absolutely unambiguous.

穆罕默德關於真主的觀念，說到底與舊約聖經的觀念相同。真主主要是權能之神；一位超越的、但強烈具有人格性的真主。祂寧可被敬畏而非被親近，真正的宗教即是服從祂的旨意（伊斯蘭教）。甚至某些蘇非派信徒似乎也不敢主張人的本性與神聖本性之完全合一。他們稱靈魂為神聖的、類似神的，但還不是真主；彷彿在此情況下形容詞真能與實體名詞區分開來，彷彿除了真主本身外還有什麼能是神聖的，彷彿還能有某種類似真主的東西，其本質卻非真主。穆罕默德對真主的哲學思辨感到厭惡。他在某處說道：「思量真主的恩慈，而非思量真主的本質。」他深知神學思辨必然導致分裂。他說道：「我的民族將分裂為七十三個宗派，其中除一個外，皆將在火獄中獲得其份額。」在穆罕默德看來，那唯一的宗派必然不會是蘇非派。
有一首有趣的詩篇，其中僕人賽義德（Said）在某個清晨首次講述他曾經歷過的一種狂喜境界。
享受，然後穆罕默德警告他不要過度的熱忱：
薩伊德發言：
「我的舌頭乾渴如火，我的血液沸騰，我的夜晚因著熾烈的愛而無眠，直到晝夜如矛掠過盾牌邊緣般急速流逝；百萬年不過一瞬。在那一刻，一切過往的永恆與一切將至的時光聚合於一個偉大的此刻——」
理解力儘管驚歎不已，凡人目睹烏雲之處，我則凝視第九重天，看見上帝的寶座。天堂與地獄對我而言盡皆赤裸，所有人類的命運亦無所遁形。
天地在我的目光中消隱，死者在我的凝視下復活。我撕開籠罩諸世界的幕紗，在天堂的宮殿中央安置我自己，炫耀如同太陽一般的光輝。
先知（穆罕默德）於是說道：友人啊，你的駿馬正在發熱；
別再催促他了。你心中的鏡子 已脫離了肉體的外殼，現在收起它吧—
再將它隱藏起來，否則你將招致危害。』
